title: Chapter 4: The Shot Put
content: Maya Okonkwo didn't trust anyone who smiled too much.

It was a rule she'd learned at fourteen, when her first coach told her she had "real potential" and then spent the next two years trying to turn her into a prom queen instead of an athlete. Smiles, in Maya's experience, were a social lubricant applied most generously by people who wanted something from you. And at Serenity Wellness Institute, everyone wanted something.
She'd been watching the new girl — Chloe — for four days now.
Chloe smiled constantly. Not the performative kind, the "I'm-healing" smile the counselors tried to teach in Group. The other kind. The kind that said *I know something you don't.*
And the things Chloe was doing didn't add up.

Day one: Chloe had asked about the cameras. Not in the paranoid way patients did — *are they watching me?* — but in a way that sounded like reconnaissance. *How many? Where's the blind spot near the laundry? Do they record audio?*

Day two: Chloe had spent an hour in the common room pretending to journal while actually mapping the hallway traffic patterns. Maya recognized the behavior because she'd done the same thing during track meets — studying the competition's warm-up routes, looking for inefficiencies in the lane assignments.
Day three: Maya had seen Chloe slip into the supply closet hallway during Group B. Ten minutes later she'd come out with her hoodie bulging in ways a hoodie shouldn't bulge.

Day four: Sophia — *Sophia*, the porcelain doll who'd spoken maybe twelve words in two months — had started sitting closer to Chloe at meals. Not close enough to be obvious. But close enough that Maya, who noticed everything, noticed.
Something was happening.
And Maya Okonkwo had spent too many years being underestimated by people who saw "Black female athlete" and stopped thinking. She wasn't about to let someone else's secret operation pass her by.
She cornered Chloe in the garden during the afternoon "mindfulness walk" — the one where they were supposed to practice being present while an orderly watched from exactly twenty feet away.
"You're up to something," Maya said.
Chloe didn't flinch. Didn't even pause her walk. "What gave it away?"
"The hoodie bulge. The hallway. The sudden alliance with the heiress." Maya fell into step beside her. "I'm a shot putter. We're trained to notice trajectories."
Chloe stopped walking. It was the first time Maya had seen her look uncertain.
"Shot putter," Chloe repeated. "That's... actually really useful."
"For throwing things."
"For knowing how to apply force to a system at exactly the right angle to make it go where you want."
Maya considered that. "That's also throwing things."
"Exactly." Chloe's smile returned. "Come to the laundry room tonight. After last meds. We'll show you what we're building."
"And if I say no?"
"Then you say no. I'm not going to pressure you." Chloe met her eyes. "But I think you've been waiting for someone to start something since the day you got here. I just happen to be the someone."
She was right. Maya hated that she was right.

The laundry room at 10:15 PM was loud enough to mask a small war. The industrial dryers ran on a cycle that nobody bothered to adjust — sheets and towels tumbling in overheated drums, the sound a relentless white-noise waterfall.

Layla was already there, cross-legged on top of a washing machine, one of the stolen tablets glowing in her lap. Sophia stood near the door, her back to the wall, her posture still perfect but her eyes different now — sharper. Less victim, more lookout.
"New recruit?" Layla asked, not looking up from the tablet.
"Maybe," Chloe said. "Maya. Two-time state shot put champion. Knows trajectories."
Layla's fingers paused. "Trajectories. That's useful."
"Why does everyone keep saying that?" Maya asked.

"Because," Layla said, turning the tablet to face her, "we need to get something out of this building without anyone seeing it. And what you know about trajectories? That's exactly how we're going to do it."

Maya looked at the screen. It showed a schematic — a top-down map of Serenity Wellness Institute, annotated in a handwriting so tiny it looked like ant tracks. Cameras marked in red. Blind spots in green. The laundry room was a green zone — one of only three in the entire building where the camera coverage failed to overlap.
"You mapped the building," Maya said.
"I mapped the building," Chloe confirmed. "Layla hacked the internal network. Sophia's been tracking staff schedules."
"Staff schedules?"

Sophia spoke for the first time. Her voice was quiet but steady. "I've been here eight months. I know when everyone eats, when they sleep, when they take their breaks. I know which orderlies check their phones during rounds. I know that Rodriguez — the night guard — has a girlfriend he calls at exactly 2:15 AM every shift."
Maya stared at her. The porcelain doll had been running counter-surveillance for eight months and nobody had noticed.
"Why didn't you do something sooner?" Maya asked.
Sophia's jaw tightened. "Because I was alone. And being alone in here isn't just lonely. It's lethal."
*SUBJECT: Serenity Wellness Institute — 23 Women Held Against Their Will*

*I am a patient at Serenity Wellness Institute in San Mateo County, California. I am not mentally ill. I was committed by my parents because I posted a TikTok criticizing my father's business practices. The video has 5.2 million views.*

*I am not alone. Of the seventeen patients currently held here, at least twelve have been committed under circumstances that suggest their "treatment" is a family reputation management service, not a medical necessity. They include:*
*A 21-year-old heiress committed for refusing to marry a 47-year-old business partner.*
*A 20-year-old Stanford engineering student committed for refusing to build a surveillance system for her father's startup.*
*A two-time state shot put champion committed for "not acting like a proper young lady."*
*They medicate us without informed consent. They monitor us 24/7. They call it wellness. I call it a compliance factory for inconvenient women.*
*I am sending this from inside the institute using their own network infrastructure. If you're reading this, please verify and publish.*
Maya finished reading. Her pulse was doing something her track coach would have called "pre-race adrenaline."
"You're going to send this."
"Already sent," Layla said. "Ten minutes ago. Before you walked in."
Maya looked at the three of them: Chloe with her weaponized smile, Layla with her ant-track handwriting, Sophia with her eight months of invisible surveillance.
"You didn't wait to see if I'd join."
"I knew you'd join," Chloe said. "You've been waiting for someone to start a fight since the day you got here. I just happened to be the someone who started one."

Maya was quiet for a long moment. The dryers rumbled. Sophia's fingers tapped against her thigh — a new habit, picked up from Layla. The red light of the hallway camera blinked through the laundry room's small window, never quite reaching the corner where they sat.
"Alright," Maya said finally. "I'm in. But I've got one condition."
"Name it."
"When this is over — when we're out — I want to be the one who throws the first punch. Metaphorically or literally. I'm not picky."
Chloe's smile widened. "Bestie," she said, "I was hoping you'd say that."

The email reached Anna Delgado's inbox at 10:47 PM Pacific Time.
Anna Delgado was a senior reporter at The Verge who had spent the last three years covering the intersection of technology, medicine, and institutional abuse. She was on her third cup of cold brew, scrolling through her inbox at a volume that would have alarmed her therapist, when she saw a subject line that made her stop scrolling.
*Serenity Wellness Institute — 23 Women Held Against Their Will*
She opened it. Read it twice. Checked the sender address — it came from an internal domain, serenitywellness.com, which meant whoever sent this was inside the building.
She picked up her phone.
"Mike?" she said when her editor answered. "Cancel my morning. I think I just found our next investigation."
In Room 214, Chloe lay on her bed, staring at the camera's red heartbeat.
"We sent the flare," she whispered to no one in particular — or maybe to the camera itself. "Now we wait."
The red light blinked.
Somewhere outside, in the city that had forgotten them, a reporter was reading their story.
And the clock, finally, had started ticking.
